title: Begin Again
text:
Begin Again.
A short practice for today.
From Lamentations 3:22-23.
"The steadfast love of the Lord never ceases, his mercies never come to an end; they are new every morning; great is your faithfulness."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Find a comfortable seat. Let the shoulders drop. Close the eyes, or soften the gaze.

Take three slow breaths. In through the nose, out through the mouth. Let the exhale be longer than the inhale.

Notice, without changing anything: the body sitting. The breath moving. The mind doing what minds do — wandering, returning, wandering, returning. This is normal. This is the practice.

Read the words slowly. The steadfast love of the Lord never ceases. His mercies are new every morning. You are not behind. You are not failing. You are simply here, in a body, in a day, in the long kindness of God.

Place a hand on your chest if that feels right. Feel the warmth. That warmth is older than you are.

When you're ready, open the eyes. Carry one word with you into the day. For me, the word is mercy. For you, it might be something else. Let it find you.

For reflection.
You do not have to carry yesterday into today. Each morning is a small mercy, handed to you without you having earned it. Begin again.

A prayer.
Lord, let this day be enough. Let the mercy that is new this morning be enough for whatever comes. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Begin Again.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Quiet Before
text:
The Quiet Before.
A short practice for today.
From Psalm 46:10.
"Be still, and know that I am God."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open. Let the eyes close.

Take one long, slow breath. Then another.

Imagine a quiet room. Not empty — quiet. The kind of room where, if you spoke, the words would land softly on the floor.

Into this room, place whatever you are carrying. Do not analyze it. Do not try to fix it. Just let it sit in the room with you.

The Psalmist says: be still, and know. Stillness is not something you achieve. It is something you stop fighting. Stop fighting for thirty seconds. That's the whole practice.

Notice: are you breathing shallow or deep? Soften the belly. Let the breath come all the way down.

Stay a little longer than feels comfortable. The discomfort is the point — it is the noise you are finally meeting.

When you open the eyes, do so slowly. The room is still there. So are you.

For reflection.
Stillness is not the absence of noise. It is the presence of something more than noise. Today, see if you can let the noise pass through you, like wind through an open window.

A prayer.
God of the quiet room, meet me here. Teach me that I do not have to fight for my worth today. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Quiet Before.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Long Exhale
text:
The Long Exhale.
A short practice for today.
From Matthew 11:28.
"Come to me, all you that are weary and are carrying heavy burdens, and I will give you rest."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit or lie down. Whichever the body wants.

Begin to lengthen the exhale. Inhale for four counts through the nose. Exhale for six or eight counts through the mouth, like a soft sigh.

Let the body soften with each exhale — the jaw, the shoulders, the hands, the belly.

The invitation today is from Jesus, to the weary, to those carrying something heavy. He does not say fix yourself first. He does not say earn it. He says come.

So come. As you are. Tired, distracted, half-believing, half-hoping. Come.

Let the breath keep lengthening. The nervous system is listening. It has been waiting for this signal — the signal that it is safe, now, to rest.

Stay with it for a few more rounds. Notice what softens. Notice what does not. Both are okay.

When you are ready, sit up slowly. You have just given yourself a small, real gift.

For reflection.
The body knows how to rest. It has been doing it your whole life. The mind sometimes forgets. The breath is a small, kind reminder.

A prayer.
Tired Christ, meet me in the tiredness. Let the rest You offer be enough. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Long Exhale.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Naming the Weight
text:
Naming the Weight.
A short practice for today.
From 1 Peter 5:7.
"Cast all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands resting.

Begin to ask yourself, very gently: what am I carrying today? Not the big abstract things. The specific ones. The email you haven't answered. The conversation you're postponing. The small dread.

Let a word or a phrase come. It doesn't have to be a complete sentence. It might be a single word — or a name, or a number, or a knot in the chest.

Now — very gently — imagine placing that word in God's hands. Not throwing it. Placing it. The way you would place a tired child on a soft bed.

Read the verse: cast all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you. The 'because' is important. The reason you can let go is not that you are strong, or that you've earned it, or that you've thought your way to peace. The reason is that He cares.

Let the breath help. A long, slow exhale is the body saying: I am handing this over, just for now.

Sit a moment longer. Notice the hands. Are they tighter or looser than when you began?

Carry the looseness with you.

For reflection.
Anxiety is the body holding a question it cannot answer. Naming the question is a kind of answer. Handing the question to God is another kind of answer.

A prayer.
Father, I name the weight: [your word here]. I do not have the strength for it today. You do. Receive it. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Naming the Weight.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Gratitude, Named
text:
Gratitude, Named.
A short practice for today.
From Colossians 3:15.
"Let the peace of Christ rule in your hearts, to which indeed you were called in the one body. And be thankful."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Soften the body.

Take three slow breaths.

Now, gently, call to mind three small things from the last twenty-four hours. Not big things. Small. The taste of a drink. The way the light came in this morning. A text you received. A moment someone laughed.

Hold each one, gently, like a small stone in your palm. There is no rush. The mind wants to move on. Let it stay a little longer with the small thing.

Notice — gratitude is not a feeling you have to manufacture. It is a way of seeing. You are not making something up. You are remembering what was always true.

Let the verse settle in: let the peace of Christ rule in your hearts. And be thankful. The peace and the thanks are not two things. They are the same gesture, from two sides.

One more small thing. One more. Let it land.

When you are ready, bow the head. A small bow, to the day. To the giver of the day.

For reflection.
Gratitude is not a feeling. It is a practice. The practice of noticing what was always there.

A prayer.
Lord, thank You for the small things I named. Thank You for the small things I cannot yet name. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Gratitude, Named.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Held
text:
Held.
A short practice for today.
From Romans 8:38-39.
"For I am convinced that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor rulers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor powers, nor height, nor depth, nor anything else in all creation, will be able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open on the knees.

Breathe in. Breathe out. The breath is a small liturgy.

Read the verse slowly. Nor death, nor life. Nor angels, nor rulers. Nor things present, nor things to come. Nor height, nor depth. The list is exhaustive on purpose.

There is no category of experience the writer forgot. There is no version of your day, your year, your lifetime, that is outside the scope of this love.

Whatever is on your mind right now — see if you can hold it inside this promise rather than the promise inside it. The promise is bigger. The promise is the room.

Let the hands rest, open. Not grasping. Not pushing away. Open. The way a hand is open when it's not trying to hold anything.

Stay a moment longer. There is nowhere else to be. Nothing else to do. Just this — being held.

When you're ready, gently close the hands. Carry the heldness with you.

For reflection.
Nothing has separated you yet. Nothing will. The love that holds you is older than your worst day.

A prayer.
Lover of my soul, thank You that I am held even when I cannot feel it. Especially then. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Held.
Be still. And begin again.

title: A Single Step
text:
A Single Step.
A short practice for today.
From Joshua 1:9.
"Be strong and courageous; do not be frightened or dismayed, for the Lord your God is with you wherever you go."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Notice the body. Notice the breath.

Bring to mind one thing you have been avoiding. Not the biggest thing. The next thing. The phone call. The email. The decision. The conversation you've been postponing.

You do not have to do it now. You are not being asked to do it now.

But see if you can name it. Just the name. Just the shape of it.

Now — see if you can take one breath into the courage you would need for the smallest version of the next step. Not the whole thing. The smallest version. The first five minutes. The first sentence.

The verse today is not a command to be unafraid. It is a promise: you are not alone in the fear. The Lord your God is with you wherever you go — including the place you have been avoiding.

Let the breath help. One breath for the fear. One breath for the next step. One breath for the promise.

You are not behind. You are not too late. The next step is enough. Today, the next step is enough.

For reflection.
Courage is rarely the big leap. It is the small, unglamorous next step, taken anyway.

A prayer.
Courageous Christ, walk with me into the thing I have been avoiding. Just the next step. Just today. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: A Single Step.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Mind, Returning
text:
The Mind, Returning.
A short practice for today.
From Philippians 4:8.
"Finally, beloved, whatever is true, whatever is honorable, whatever is just, whatever is pure, whatever is lovely, whatever is gracious, if there is any excellence and if there is anything worthy of praise, think about these things."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands at rest.

Set a timer for three minutes. For three minutes, your only job is to follow the breath.

In. Out. The mind will leave. It always does. Notice, without judgment, that it has left. Gently bring it back. Like calling a small child by name. Not harshly. Just: there you are.

The returning is the practice. There is no version of this where the mind behaves. There is only the version where you keep returning.

When the timer ends, notice: you have just practiced the muscle that Paul is asking for. Whatever is true, whatever is lovely. The mind can be trained — not by force, but by gentle, repeated, patient return.

The breath is the training partner. Use it today whenever the mind goes somewhere you'd rather it didn't. One breath. One return. That's enough.

Carry the practice out the door with you.

For reflection.
The mind wanders a thousand times an hour. The practice is not to stop the wandering. The practice is the gentle return.

A prayer.
Lord of the returning mind, meet me each time I come back. Especially then. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Mind, Returning.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Green Pasture
text:
The Green Pasture.
A short practice for today.
From Psalm 23:1-3.
"The Lord is my shepherd, I shall not want. He makes me lie down in green pastures; he leads me beside still waters; he restores my soul."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

If you can, lie down. If not, sit with the eyes closed and imagine lying in a wide, soft place.

The Psalmist is not writing about a personality. He is writing about a posture. The posture of being led. Of not having to be the one in charge, just for now.

He makes me lie down. Notice — the shepherd does not ask. The sheep do not lie down on their own. They have to be led to a place where the body says: it is safe now. You can rest.

Where in your life is the shepherd trying to lead you to a green pasture, but you keep standing up? Where is the body being told: just lie down here, just for a moment?

Let yourself be led. Let the breath be the still water. Drink slowly.

He restores my soul. Restoration is not a productivity hack. It is the slow, kind work of being brought back to yourself.

Stay a few more minutes. The pasture is not going anywhere. Neither is the shepherd.

For reflection.
Sometimes the most spiritual act is to lie down. To be led. To stop producing.

A prayer.
Good Shepherd, lead me to the still water. I will not argue today. I will lie down. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Green Pasture.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Quiet "Yes"
text:
The Quiet "Yes".
A short practice for today.
From 1 Samuel 3:10.
"And the Lord came and stood there, calling as before, 'Samuel! Samuel!' And Samuel said, 'Speak, for your servant is listening.'"

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open. Eyes closed.

Take three slow breaths. Each exhale, a little longer than the inhale.

Now — for a few moments — just listen. Not to the world. To whatever is underneath the world. To the small, still voice that has been there all along, underneath the noise.

Samuel's answer is one of the most beautiful in all of scripture. Speak, for your servant is listening. He does not say: tell me what to do. He does not say: solve my problem. He says: I am here. I am listening. Use me.

That posture — open, listening, available — is the whole practice. You don't have to hear anything specific. You don't have to figure it out. You just have to be in the posture.

What if today, every time you pause, you say that small sentence? Speak, Lord, for your servant is listening. Let it become a refrain. A breathing prayer.

Stay a few more minutes. Whatever you hear — a word, an image, a quiet — let it land softly.

For reflection.
The most spiritual word in the Bible is sometimes a small, quiet 'yes' — followed by listening.

A prayer.
Speak, Lord. Your servant is listening. Even now. Especially now. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Quiet "Yes".
Be still. And begin again.

title: Peace, Not as the World Gives
text:
Peace, Not as the World Gives.
A short practice for today.
From John 14:27.
"Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. I do not give to you as the world gives. Do not let your hearts be troubled, and do not let them be afraid."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Notice the body. Notice the breath.

Read the verse slowly. Especially this part: I do not give to you as the world gives.

There is a different kind of peace being offered. Not the peace of everything going right. The peace of something going right inside you, even when nothing outside is going right.

Let the breath become the prayer. In — I receive the peace. Out — I let go of the trouble.

Stay a few more minutes. The peace is patient.

For reflection.
The world's peace is the absence of trouble. Christ's peace is the presence of something stronger than trouble.

A prayer.
Prince of Peace, I receive Your peace today — the kind that does not depend on the day. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Peace, Not as the World Gives.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Do Not Worry About Tomorrow
text:
Do Not Worry About Tomorrow.
A short practice for today.
From Matthew 6:34.
"Do not worry about tomorrow, for tomorrow will bring worries of its own. Today's trouble is enough for today."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands resting. Shoulders soft.

Read the verse once. Then again, more slowly. Today's trouble is enough for today.

Worry is the mind doing laps. The body sitting still while the mind runs a marathon. Today, we step off the track.

One breath. In. Out. If a thought about tomorrow comes, see it, name it — that's a tomorrow thought — and gently return. You are not dismissing it. You are just letting it be a thought, not a tyrant.

Carry this small practice out the door. The whole of today is enough.

For reflection.
Tomorrow is a country you have not yet visited. Worrying about it is the slowest form of travel.

A prayer.
Lord of today, deliver me from the tyranny of tomorrow. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Do Not Worry About Tomorrow.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Power in Weakness
text:
Power in Weakness.
A short practice for today.
From 2 Corinthians 12:9.
"My grace is sufficient for you, for power is made perfect in weakness."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Notice where the body feels tired today. Do not try to fix it. Just notice.

Read the verse slowly. Power is made perfect in weakness. Not in spite of weakness. Through it.

The world's logic says: get stronger, then you can do the thing. The gospel's logic says: be honest about the weakness, and see what shows up.

This is the most counter-intuitive form of courage: the courage to say, today, I cannot do this alone. The courage to be small.

Let the breath soften. Let the shoulders drop. The grace does not arrive after the weakness is gone. The grace arrives now.

For reflection.
Weakness is not the opposite of grace. Weakness is the place where grace has room to land.

A prayer.
Lord, let my weakness be the doorway through which Your grace enters. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Power in Weakness.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Kindness, First
text:
Kindness, First.
A short practice for today.
From Ephesians 4:32.
"Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open. Soften the face.

Read the verse slowly. Be kind. Tenderhearted. Forgiving. As God in Christ forgave you.

Notice the structure: it does not say, be kind when they deserve it. Be kind — the way you have been forgiven.

So kindness is not a strategy. It is a memory. The memory of being forgiven, of being received, of being loved without having earned it.

You will meet people today. Strangers, perhaps. Be kind to them — not because they have earned it, but because you have been shown a kindness that does not run out.

For reflection.
Kindness is rarely loud. It is the small, soft gesture that the loud world does not even notice.

A prayer.
Lord, let me be kind today, even in the small ways. Especially in the small ways. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Kindness, First.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Near to the Brokenhearted
text:
Near to the Brokenhearted.
A short practice for today.
From Psalm 34:18.
"The Lord is near to the brokenhearted, and saves the crushed in spirit."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit, or lie down. Whichever the body wants.

If today is a hard day, do not try to be brave. Let the body feel what it feels. Let the tears come if they come.

The verse does not promise you will not be broken. It promises the Lord is near to the broken. Near. Not solving. Not fixing. Near.

Imagine someone sitting next to you. Not saying anything. Just sitting. That is the image.

Place a hand on your chest. Feel the heartbeat. That is the Lord's nearness, in pulse form.

Stay as long as you need.

For reflection.
If you are broken today, this verse is for you. Especially then, the Lord is near.

A prayer.
Lord, I am broken today. Meet me here. Be near. That is enough. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Near to the Brokenhearted.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Trust, Not Sight
text:
Trust, Not Sight.
A short practice for today.
From Proverbs 3:5-6.
"Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and do not rely on your own insight. In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands at rest.

Read the verse. Trust in the Lord with all your heart. Not with the part of you that has it all figured out. With all of it — including the part that is confused, the part that doubts.

Do not rely on your own insight. The verse is not anti-thinking. It is just saying: thinking is not enough. There is something older, deeper, more reliable than your own reasoning.

Let the breath be the place where you stop trying to figure it out. In. Out. The breath does not plan. The breath just arrives, and is enough.

For reflection.
Trust is the muscle that atrophies fastest in a noisy world. Today, we stretch it gently.

A prayer.
Lord, I do not have the answers today. Help me to trust the One who does. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Trust, Not Sight.
Be still. And begin again.

title: A Gift, Not a Wage
text:
A Gift, Not a Wage.
A short practice for today.
From Ephesians 2:8-9.
"For by grace you have been saved through faith, and this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open.

Read the verse slowly. It is the gift of God. Not the result of works.

What would today look like if you stopped earning what has already been given? What would the next hour look like? The next decision?

If you are like most people, much of your day is a small, quiet performance. The verse is interrupting that performance. You are already enough. The gift has already been given. There is nothing to earn.

Let the breath carry the relief. The body understands gifts better than the mind does.

For reflection.
The most Christian thing in the world is that you cannot earn what you have already been given.

A prayer.
Lord, I receive. I do not earn. I do not perform. I receive. Thank You. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: A Gift, Not a Wage.
Be still. And begin again.

title: A Renewed Mind
text:
A Renewed Mind.
A short practice for today.
From Romans 12:2.
"Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your minds."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Notice the mind. No judgment.

The mind is doing its mind thing — thinking, planning, rehearsing. This is what minds do. The verse is not asking you to stop the mind. It is asking you to give it a new shape.

Renewing is slow. It is not a renovation. It is a slow, patient cultivation. The gardener does not rip out the weeds in anger. She pulls one, then another.

Today, just notice one thought that has been returning uninvited. Do not argue with it. Just see it. See it as a thought, not a truth.

The breath is the place where thoughts are seen, not obeyed.

For reflection.
The mind is not a thing to be mastered. It is a garden to be tended. Today, we just pull one small weed.

A prayer.
Lord, renew my mind — one breath, one thought at a time. Be patient with me. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: A Renewed Mind.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Let the Peace Rule
text:
Let the Peace Rule.
A short practice for today.
From Colossians 3:15.
"Let the peace of Christ rule in your hearts. And be thankful."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open. Soften the face.

Read the verse twice. Let the peace of Christ rule. Rule, not visit. Rule.

What would it look like to let the peace rule — in the small decisions of the day? The decision to pause. The decision to look up instead of down.

And: be thankful. The gratitude is not a separate step. It is the atmosphere in which the peace can be felt.

Notice three small things you are thankful for, right now. The breath. The chair. The friend.

For reflection.
Gratitude is the atmosphere in which the peace of Christ can be felt.

A prayer.
Lord, thank You. Let the peace rule in me today. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Let the Peace Rule.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Hand That Holds
text:
The Hand That Holds.
A short practice for today.
From Isaiah 41:10.
"Do not fear, for I am with you. I will strengthen you, I will help you, I will uphold you with my victorious right hand."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands on the knees, palms up. Open.

Read the verse, very slowly. I will strengthen you. I will help you. I will uphold you. Notice the threefold promise.

If you are afraid today — of something specific, or something vague, or something you cannot name — the verse is not asking you to stop being afraid. It is asking you to notice who is with you in the fear.

Imagine a hand. Not yours. A larger, older hand, holding yours. It does not have to be visible. The hand is there.

When you stand up, take the hand with you.

For reflection.
You do not have to be strong enough on your own. The hand is already there.

A prayer.
Lord, I am afraid. Be with me in the fear. I do not have to be unafraid. I only have to be held. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Hand That Holds.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Quietness and Trust
text:
Quietness and Trust.
A short practice for today.
From Isaiah 30:15.
"In returning and rest you shall be saved; in quietness and in trust shall be your strength."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Or lie down. Whatever the body needs.

Read the verse twice. In returning and rest. In quietness and in trust.

The verse is not asking you to be loud in your faith. It is asking you to be still in it. To let the strength come from somewhere other than your own effort.

Notice how much of your day is a kind of striving. A doing. A making-it-happen. The verse is offering a different way: in returning, in rest, in quietness, in trust.

Let the breath slow. Let the body remember that strength is not always in the muscles. Sometimes it is in the willingness to be held.

Carry this with you today. The quiet way. The trust way.

For reflection.
Strength is often a quieter thing than we were taught. It is sometimes just the willingness to stop.

A prayer.
Lord, teach me the strength of quietness today. Teach me to stop, and to trust. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Quietness and Trust.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Fruit, in Season
text:
The Fruit, in Season.
A short practice for today.
From Galatians 5:22-23.
"The fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, generosity, faithfulness, gentleness, and self-control."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open. Read the verse slowly.

Love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, generosity, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control. Read each word as a small gift, not a demand.

These are not qualities you are required to produce. They are fruit. And fruit grows in its own time, in the right conditions.

The conditions are these: staying close to the Vine. Staying in the quiet. Letting the gardener do the pruning and the watering.

Which of these words is most needed today? Love, if you are tired. Patience, if you are frustrated. Kindness, if you are angry. Self-control, if you are overwhelmed.

Pick one. Carry it. The others will come in their season.

For reflection.
Fruit does not grow on demand. It grows in season, slowly, in the dark of the soil. So do we.

A prayer.
Lord, make me a fruitful branch. Today, especially, grow in me the fruit I most need. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Fruit, in Season.
Be still. And begin again.

title: I Can, Through Him
text:
I Can, Through Him.
A short practice for today.
From Philippians 4:13.
"I can do all things through him who strengthens me."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Notice the body.

Read the verse. I can do all things through him who strengthens me.

It is tempting to read this as a superhero verse — the verse for accomplishing anything. But that is not what Paul meant. He was writing from prison, having lost almost everything. All things, for him, was the next small thing. The next letter. The next conversation. The next breath.

Today, the verse is for the next small thing. Not the whole day. Not the whole life. The next small thing.

Let the breath be the place where the strength arrives. In — I receive. Out — I let go.

The strength is not yours. It is the One who strengthens. The verse does not say I am strong. It says I can do all things through him who strengthens me.

Carry that distinction with you today. You are not the source. You are the channel.

For reflection.
All things does not mean everything. It means the things you are actually being asked to do today. The next small thing.

A prayer.
Lord, give me strength for the next small thing. The rest is in Your hands. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: I Can, Through Him.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Joy Comes in the Morning
text:
Joy Comes in the Morning.
A short practice for today.
From Psalm 30:5.
"Weeping may linger for the night, but joy comes with the morning."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Let the body feel what it feels.

Read the verse. Weeping may linger for the night, but joy comes with the morning.

The verse is not dismissing the night. It is not saying cheer up. It is saying: the night is real, and the morning is coming.

Where are you today? In the night? In the morning? Somewhere in between?

If you are in the night, hear this: the morning is not your job. You do not have to make the morning come. The morning is promised. Your only job tonight is to not give up on the dawn.

If you are in the morning, give thanks. The night passed. You are still here. That is the joy.

Either way, sit. Breathe. The verse is true. Morning is on its way.

For reflection.
If it is night now, morning is coming. If it is morning, you have already been carried through.

A prayer.
Lord of morning, meet me in the night. Carry me until the dawn. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Joy Comes in the Morning.
Be still. And begin again.

title: A Very Present Help
text:
A Very Present Help.
A short practice for today.
From Psalm 46:1.
"God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands on the chest, if that feels right.

Read the verse. God is our refuge and strength, a very present help in trouble.

Very present. Not eventually present. Not after you have sorted yourself out. Present. Right now. In the trouble, not after it.

What is the trouble today? Name it, if you can. Just a word. Just a shape. The verse does not promise the trouble will go away. It promises Someone is in it with you.

Refuge is not escape. Refuge is a place to stand, a place to breathe, while the trouble moves around you.

Let the breath help. In: I am not alone. Out: I do not have to do this alone.

Stay a few more minutes. The help is very present. It is not going anywhere.

For reflection.
Not a distant help. A very present help. The kind that is here, right now, before you have even asked.

A prayer.
Lord, be my refuge today. I will stand here, with You, in the trouble. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: A Very Present Help.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Where Two or Three
text:
Where Two or Three.
A short practice for today.
From Matthew 18:20.
"Where two or three are gathered in my name, I am there among them."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands open. Read the verse slowly.

Where two or three are gathered in my name, I am there among them.

The verse is a quiet revolution. It says: you do not need a cathedral. You do not need a stadium. You do not need a perfect congregation. Two or three. That is the threshold.

Who are the two or three in your life today? The friend you are going to call. The person you are going to sit with. Even the stranger you are going to smile at — you are gathering.

You do not have to do this alone. That is the whole invitation.

Let the breath be a small gathering. In: the One. Out: you. The One and you. The two. The temple.

Carry this with you. The presence is in the small gathering, not the large crowd.

For reflection.
Even the smallest gathering — two, three — is a temple. The presence is not waiting for a crowd.

A prayer.
Lord, thank You for being in the small things. In the two, in the three. In me. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Where Two or Three.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Face That Shines
text:
The Face That Shines.
A short practice for today.
From Numbers 6:24-25.
"The Lord bless you and keep you; the Lord make his face to shine upon you, and be gracious to you."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands on the knees, palms up. Open.

Read the verse slowly. The Lord bless you and keep you. The Lord make his face to shine upon you. Be gracious to you.

This is one of the oldest blessings in the world. Older than the morning, older than you, older than your worry.

The verse is a small window into how you are seen. Not as a project to be fixed. Not as a problem to be solved. As someone upon whom a face is shining. With grace. With kindness. With the long, slow tenderness of the One who made you.

Let that be the truth underneath all the other truths today. Beneath the work, beneath the family, beneath the noise, beneath the small daily failures: a face is shining upon you. With grace.

Stay a few more minutes. The face is not going anywhere. Let it find you.

For reflection.
Some blessings are old. Some are older than the morning. Let them find you today.

A prayer.
Lord, let Your face shine upon me. I receive the blessing, old as it is, true as it is. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Face That Shines.
Be still. And begin again.

title: The Light of the World
text:
The Light of the World.
A short practice for today.
From Matthew 5:14.
"You are the light of the world. A city built on a hill cannot be hid."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands at rest. Notice the breath.

Read the verse. You are the light of the world.

The verse is not a demand to be brighter. It is a recognition. The light is already there. The verse is not asking you to generate the light. It is asking you to stop covering it up.

What covers your light today? The fear of being seen. The fear of being wrong. The small, daily habits of hiding. The verse is asking, gently, to put those down.

Let the breath be the place where the covering thins. In: I am the light. Out: I do not have to hide it.

You are not the source of the light. You are the window. The light is on the other side. Your only job is to stay clean enough to let it through.

Carry this with you. The light is not yours to generate. It is yours to reveal.

For reflection.
You do not have to make yourself shine. You only have to stop hiding.

A prayer.
Lord, let Your light come through me today. I will stop hiding it. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: The Light of the World.
Be still. And begin again.

title: Everlasting Love
text:
Everlasting Love.
A short practice for today.
From Jeremiah 31:3.
"I have loved you with an everlasting love; I have drawn you with loving-kindness."

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands on the chest. Eyes closed.

Read the verse, very slowly. I have loved you with an everlasting love.

Everlasting. The love is not new. It is not contingent on your performance. It is not a reward for getting it right. It is older than your doubt, older than your failure, older than the morning.

I have drawn you. Not pushed. Not demanded. Drawn. The way the sun draws a flower. The way the tide draws the shore. Slowly. Gently. Without force.

You are not being chased. You are being drawn. There is a difference. The chase makes you run. The drawing lets you be still, and come toward.

Let the breath be the drawing. In: the love that is everlasting. Out: the resistance, let it go.

Stay a few more minutes. The drawing is not finished. The love is not finished. They are still in motion.

For reflection.
Everlasting means before. Before your doubt, before your failure, before the morning.

A prayer.
Lord, I receive the everlasting love. I stop running. I let myself be drawn. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: Everlasting Love.
Be still. And begin again.

title: My Light, My Salvation
text:
My Light, My Salvation.
A short practice for today.
From Psalm 27:1.
"The Lord is my light and my salvation; whom shall I fear? The Lord is the stronghold of my life; of whom shall I be afraid?"

Find a comfortable position. Let the eyes close, or soften the gaze.
Take three slow breaths.

Sit. Hands at rest. Read the verse. Twice.

The Lord is my light and my salvation. Whom shall I fear?

The Lord is the stronghold of my life. Of whom shall I be afraid?

Notice the questions. They are not really questions. They are answers, looking for the agreement of your heart. Of whom shall I be afraid. The answer the verse expects is: no one. Of no one.

Not because there is nothing to be afraid of. There is. But because the One who is your light is bigger than the fear.

Let the breath be the place where the answer lands. In: the Lord is my light. Out: whom shall I fear?

You are not the source of the courage. The One who is your light is the source. The courage is borrowed. The fear is real. The borrowed courage is bigger.

Carry this with you today. Whom shall I fear. The answer is no one.

For reflection.
The question is rhetorical. The answer is no one. The One who is your light is bigger than the fear.

A prayer.
Lord, You are my light. The fear is real, but You are bigger. I will not be afraid today. Amen.

Carry one word with you into the day.
The word is: My Light, My Salvation.
Be still. And begin again.
